lesson:
The kanji 始 is 女 (woman) + 台 (platform, base). The connection to "begin" traces back to the idea of a woman as the origin of life — birth as the first beginning. The same kanji appears in 原始 (げんし, primitive/primordial) and 始祖 (しそ, founder/ancestor), always carrying that sense of original starting point.

始める is transitive — you start something. 仕事を始める (start work). 勉強を始める (start studying). Its intransitive partner is 始まる (はじまる) — something starts on its own: 映画が始まる (the movie starts). This is a classic える/ある transitive/intransitive pair, and you'll see the pattern again and again: 終える/終わる, 変える/変わる, 決める/決まる.

Key pattern: 〜始める attached to a verb's masu-stem means "start doing." 雨が降り始めた (it started raining). 読み始める (start reading). This is the mirror image of 〜終わる (finish doing). Together they bracket any action.

Particles: 始める takes を — the thing you start is your direct object. 新しい生活を始める (start a new life).

Potential が始められる: が marks what can be started. 来月から授業が始められる (classes can be started from next month).

Passive に始められる: に marks who started it. 先に始められた (they started before me).

Causative に始めさせる: に marks who you make start. 生徒に練習を始めさせる (have the students start practice).

meaning: to wake, get up; to occur
lesson:
The kanji 起 is 走 (run) + 己 (self). The self, running — rising up and getting moving. That captures both meanings: physically waking up (your body rises), and events occurring (things rise into being). The motion is always upward, from horizontal to vertical, from dormant to active.

朝早く起きる (wake up early in the morning). 事故が起きた (an accident occurred). 地震が起きる (an earthquake happens). The "wake up" meaning is for people. The "occur" meaning is for events, problems, phenomena. You won't confuse them because the subject tells you which is which — a person wakes up, a situation arises.

起きる vs 起こる vs 起こす: Three related verbs. 起きる is intransitive — you wake up, things happen. 起こる (おこる) is also intransitive but only means "occur" — it doesn't mean "wake up." 起こす (おこす) is transitive — you wake someone up, you cause something to happen. 子供を起こす (wake the kid up). 問題を起こす (cause a problem).

Particles: 起きる takes が for the subject — the person who wakes or the event that occurs. 子供が起きた (the kid woke up). No direct object — it's intransitive. For time, use に: 六時に起きる (wake up at six).

Passive: not commonly used — waking up is something you do, not something done to you. For events, you'd say 起こされた (the transitive causative) if someone caused it.

Causative を／に起きさせる: making someone wake up. 子供を起きさせる (make the kid get up). In practice, 起こす (the transitive pair) is more natural for "wake someone up."

The kanji 春 has 日 (sun) at the bottom and three strokes on top that originally represented emerging plants. Sun warming the earth, making things push upward — spring. In the oldest Chinese script, the character was more explicitly a picture of seedlings sprouting under sunlight. What survived is still legible if you squint: the energy of growth rising toward warmth.

はる is deeply woven into Japanese culture in a way that no other season quite matches. 春 is cherry blossom season, the start of the school year, the fiscal year, new jobs, fresh starts. While Western new beginnings cluster around January, Japanese ones cluster around April. 春休み (はるやすみ, spring break) marks the boundary between school years — it's not a vacation in the middle of the year, it's the transition between endings and new beginnings.

The word はる may be related to the verb 張る (はる, to swell/stretch) — the season when buds swell and burst open. Some linguists also connect it to 墾る (はる, to plow/clear land), since spring is when farmers begin working the fields. Either etymology roots the word in physical expansion — things opening up after winter's constriction.

午前 is the mirror of 午後: 午 (noon) + 前 (before) = "before noon." Together with 午後, they split the day at its midpoint. Clean, symmetrical, borrowed wholesale from the Chinese system of dividing time by the zodiac hours.

In usage, 午前 goes before the time: 午前十時 (10 AM). But here's something worth noticing: Japanese people don't always say 午前 for "morning" in casual speech. They're more likely to say 朝 (あさ) for early morning or just use context. 午前 is the precise, clock-oriented word — schedules, appointments, news broadcasts. 朝 is the lived experience of morning — waking up, the commute, breakfast.

午前中 (ごぜんちゅう) means "during the morning" or "throughout the morning" and is extremely useful: 午前中に届きます (it'll arrive sometime in the morning). The 中 adds a sense of "somewhere within that span" — no exact time, just the general morning window.

The kanji 別 has a blade (刂, the knife radical) on the right — cutting something apart, separating. The left side (另) originally depicted a mouth and a bone, suggesting the act of cutting flesh from bone. Separation at its most visceral. This kanji shows up in 別れる (わかれる, to part/break up), 区別 (くべつ, distinction), 特別 (とくべつ, special — literally "especially separate").

As a な-adjective, 別 means "separate" or "different" and goes before nouns with な: 別な問題 (a different problem). But it also frequently appears without な, acting more like a noun: 別の話 (a different topic). Both 別な and 別の are correct and common — 別の is slightly more frequent in modern speech.

別に as an adverb is one of those words you'll hear constantly in casual Japanese. 別にいい (I don't really care / it's fine). 別に何もない (nothing in particular). It carries a dismissive, slightly indifferent tone — "not especially," "not particularly." In anime and drama, 別に... followed by a trailing-off is the classic tsundere response: pretending not to care.

どこ is the question member of the ko-so-a-do place series: ここ (here), そこ (there, near you), あそこ (over there), どこ (where?). The system is perfectly regular — swap the first syllable and the meaning changes. You already know the logic from これ/それ/あれ/どれ.

どこに行くの？ (where are you going?). トイレはどこですか (where's the bathroom?). どこから来ましたか (where are you from?). The particle after どこ shapes the question: に asks where you're heading, で asks where something happens, から asks where something comes from.

Like all question words in Japanese, どこ shapeshifts with particles: どこか (somewhere), どこも (everywhere / nowhere, depending on positive or negative), どこでも (anywhere). The pattern is identical to いつか/いつも/いつでも — learn one question word's particle combinations and you've learned them all.

Two kanji: 部 (section, department) + 屋 (roof, shop, house). A sectioned-off space under a roof — a room. The reading へや is irregular; you'd expect ぶや from the standard readings, but this compound took its own path. Just memorize it as a unit.

屋 on its own is worth knowing — it appears everywhere. When it means "roof/building," it's read や: 本屋 (ほんや, bookstore), 花屋 (はなや, florist), パン屋 (bakery). It turns any product into the shop that sells it. The kanji itself shows a corpse radical (尸) — originally a person lying down — over a reaching gesture, representing a person sheltering under a roof. A body at rest under cover.

部屋 is the default word for any room in a building — your bedroom, a hotel room, a meeting room. 部屋に入る (enter a room). 部屋を片付ける (tidy up the room). In sumo, 部屋 also means a wrestling stable — the place where wrestlers live and train together. 相撲部屋 (すもうべや) is a compound you'll encounter if you follow sumo at all.

The kanji 若 has 艹 (grass radical) on top — young shoots, tender growth. The bottom part 右 was borrowed for its phonetic value, but the visual tells the story: new green growth. Something fresh, not yet mature. In Buddhist terminology, 般若 (はんにゃ) uses 若 for a completely different meaning (wisdom), a reminder that many kanji have wildly different lives in different contexts.

若い usually describes people, but it stretches to anything that seems fresh or unripe. 若い人 (a young person). 若い頃 (when I was young). まだ若い (still young). You can also hear 若く見える (look young) — a compliment in any culture, but especially effective in Japan.

Compounds: 若者 (わかもの, young people) is a common noun — you'll see it in news articles about generational trends. 若手 (わかて, a young/junior person) is used in workplaces and professions to describe someone still rising. Both treat youth not as an age but as a stage — not yet fully developed, still proving yourself.

The kanji 車 is a top-down view of a cart: the axle running through the middle, two wheels on either side. This is one of the most faithful kanji-to-picture correspondences that survived from ancient script. Oracle bone versions from 3,500 years ago show a two-wheeled chariot so clearly you could build one from the drawing. The modern character is a simplification, but the bones are there — a central shaft with horizontal elements crossing it.

Originally 車 meant any wheeled vehicle — carts, carriages, rickshaws. In modern Japanese, it defaults to "car" in most contexts. 車で行く (go by car). 車を運転する (drive a car). But the older meaning survives in compounds: 電車 (でんしゃ, electric train), 自転車 (じてんしゃ, bicycle — literally "self-rotating vehicle"), 風車 (ふうしゃ, windmill). Any machine with wheels or rotating parts can claim 車.

The word くるま itself likely comes from くる (to spin) + ま (round thing) — the spinning round thing. A wheel. Then the thing with wheels. Then the car.

The kanji 置 has 网 (net) on top and 直 (straight, direct) on the bottom. The idea: setting a net down in a fixed position — placing something deliberately. 置く isn't about tossing something down casually. It implies intention — you're putting something where it belongs, or at least where you want it.

テーブルに置く (put on the table). 本棚に置く (place on the bookshelf). 鍵をどこに置いた？ (where did I put the keys?). The particle に marks the location — where the thing ends up — and を marks the thing being placed.

Grammar pattern: 〜ておく (often contracted to 〜とく in casual speech) means to do something in advance, as preparation. 買っておく (buy in advance). 調べておく (look it up beforehand). そのままにしておく (leave it as it is — a deliberate decision to not change something). The image is placing the action down for later use — doing it now so it's ready when you need it. This is one of the most useful grammar patterns in spoken Japanese.

Particles: 置く takes を for the thing placed and に for the location. 荷物をここに置く (put the luggage here).

Potential が置ける: が marks what can be placed. ここに車が置ける (you can park a car here).

Passive に置かれる: に marks who placed it. 変な場所に置かれた (it was placed in a weird spot).

Causative に…を置かせる: に marks who you make put something down. 子供に荷物を置かせる (have the kid put down the bags).

The kanji 住 is 亻 (person) + 主 (master, main). A person who is the master of a place — someone settled, rooted. Not passing through. 住む means to live somewhere as your base, your home. It's not about being alive (that's 生きる) and it's not about temporarily staying (that's 泊まる). It's about where your life is anchored.

Almost always used with に for the place and ている for the ongoing state: 東京に住んでいる (I live in Tokyo). Like 知る, the dictionary form 住む describes the act of settling somewhere, while 住んでいる describes the state of living there. You'll almost always want the ている form.

住 feeds into useful compounds: 住所 (じゅうしょ, address), 住民 (じゅうみん, resident), 住宅 (じゅうたく, residence/housing). The on'yomi じゅう appears in all formal/administrative contexts. The kun'yomi すむ stays in everyday speech.

Particles: 住む takes に — the place where you live. 日本に住む (live in Japan). に marks the location of residence, the place you're rooted to.

Passive に住まれる: uncommon, but possible in indirect passive — 隣に変な人に住まれて困っている (a weird person lives next door and it's a problem for me).

Causative を／に住ませる: making or letting someone live somewhere. に for permissive — 学生にこのアパートに住ませる (let students live in this apartment).

働 is rare among kanji — it was invented in Japan, not imported from China. It's a 国字 (こくじ, national character), combining 亻 (person) + 動 (move). A person in motion. That's the Japanese definition of work: a human being moving, doing, physically engaging with the world. The Chinese character for work (工) is abstract — a beam or tool. The Japanese invention is embodied — it's about the worker, not the work.

働く is intransitive — you work, but you don't "work" an object. The place where you work takes で: 会社で働く (work at a company). The field takes で too: IT業界で働いている (I work in the IT industry). Like 住む, you'll most often use the ている form to describe your ongoing work situation.

働く can also mean "to function" or "to operate" for non-humans: 頭が働かない (my brain isn't working). 薬が働く (the medicine takes effect). And in a darker sense: 悪事を働く (commit a crime) — here 働く unusually takes を, treating the wrongdoing as the object of labor.

Particles: 働く takes で for the workplace — 病院で働く (work at a hospital). で marks where the action happens. For the company or organization specifically, に also works: トヨタに働いている.

Passive に働かれる: indirect passive — someone working affects you. Rare in natural speech.

Causative を／に働かせる: making someone work. を for coercive — 社員を休みなく働かせる (make employees work without days off). A word you see in discussions about labor rights.

The kanji 難 is dense — 隹 (short-tailed bird) on the right and a complex left component that originally depicted a clay vessel near fire. The ancient meaning was "a bird that's hard to catch" or more broadly, "hardship, disaster." The character still carries both senses: 難しい (difficult) and 災難 (さいなん, disaster). Difficulty and suffering share a kanji because the ancient Chinese saw them as the same thing.

むずかしい describes intellectual or situational difficulty — a hard test, a complex problem, a tricky person. 日本語は難しい (Japanese is hard). 難しい顔をする (to make a difficult face — to look troubled or stern). That last expression is revealing: 難しい can describe people who are hard to deal with — picky, demanding, or just socially complicated.

難しい vs 大変: Both can mean "hard" but from different angles. 難しい is about complexity — a puzzle, a skill, a concept. 大変 (たいへん) is about the scale of effort or trouble — an overwhelming workload, a terrible situation. A math problem is 難しい. Moving house while working full-time is 大変. One challenges your brain. The other exhausts your everything.

先 (before, ahead) + 生 (born, life) = someone born before you, someone who came first. That's the literal meaning, and it explains why 先生 extends far beyond classroom teachers. Doctors are 先生. Lawyers are 先生. Authors, politicians, martial arts masters — all 先生. The word honors anyone who has knowledge or authority that came from getting there first.

In practice, 先生 functions both as a noun ("a teacher") and as a title ("Doctor Tanaka" = 田中先生). When used as a title, it replaces さん — you don't say 田中先生さん. And you can address someone directly as just 先生, the way English uses "Doctor" or "Professor" standalone.

There's an ironic usage too. Sarcastically calling someone 先生 — especially in the third person — implies they're being arrogant or self-important. あの先生は偉そうだね can mean the person acts like they know everything. The same word that conveys deep respect can, with the right tone, convey the opposite.

The kanji 立 is a person standing on the ground. Two legs on a horizontal line. It's been this picture for over three thousand years — one of the most stable, recognizable kanji across the entire history of the writing system. A human being on their feet, vertical, present.

立つ is intransitive — you stand up, you rise, something stands. ここに立って (stand here). 椅子から立つ (stand up from a chair). Its transitive partner is 立てる (たてる) — to set something upright, to establish, to formulate: 計画を立てる (make a plan — stand it up). 旗を立てる (raise a flag).

立つ has a rich idiomatic life. 役に立つ (to be useful — to stand in service). 腹が立つ (to get angry — your belly rises). 目立つ (to stand out — the eyes stand, drawn to something). 立場 (たちば, standpoint/position). The vertical image runs through all of them — being upright means being visible, active, and present.

Particles: 立つ takes に for where you stand — 入口に立つ (stand at the entrance). For rising from somewhere, から: 席から立つ (stand up from your seat).

Passive に立たれる: indirect passive — someone standing somewhere affects you. 前に立たれて見えない (someone stood in front of me and I can't see).

Causative を／に立たせる: making someone stand. を for coercive — 生徒を廊下に立たせる (make a student stand in the hallway — a classic Japanese school punishment).

The kanji 呼 has 口 (mouth) on the left and 乎 (an exclamation) on the right. A mouth crying out — calling to someone. The character's original sense was exhaling forcefully, shouting, summoning. It's the opposite of 吸 (すう, to inhale/suck in), and together they form 呼吸 (こきゅう, breathing — out and in).

呼ぶ covers both "to call out to someone" and "to call someone something." 名前を呼ぶ (call someone's name). タクシーを呼ぶ (call a taxi). 友達を呼ぶ (invite friends over — call them to come). 彼を「先生」と呼ぶ (call him "sensei"). The particle と frames the name or title, just like with 言う.

Notice that 呼ぶ can mean "invite" — calling someone to a place implies you want them there. パーティーに呼ぶ (invite to a party). The word doesn't draw a hard line between summoning and inviting. Whether it's a command or a welcome depends entirely on the relationship and context.

Particles: 呼ぶ takes を for the person called and と for what you call them. 彼を「田中さん」と呼ぶ (call him "Tanaka-san").

Potential が呼べる: が marks who can be called. ここからタクシーが呼べる (you can call a taxi from here).

Passive に呼ばれる: に marks who calls you. 先生に呼ばれた (I was called by the teacher). Very common — being summoned is a frequent experience.

Causative に…を呼ばせる: に marks who you make do the calling. 子供に友達を呼ばせる (have the kid call their friends).

大 (big) + 学 (study) = big study. Grand learning. In the Confucian tradition, 大学 was one of the Four Books — a foundational text about self-cultivation and governance. The word carried philosophical weight long before it meant an institution with a campus and tuition. A 大学 was supposed to be where you pursued the "great learning" — not just skills, but wisdom.

In modern Japan, 大学 is where the real social sorting happens. Which university you attend — 東大 (とうだい, University of Tokyo), 京大 (きょうだい, Kyoto University), 早稲田, 慶応 — often matters more for your career than what you study there. 大学に入る (enter university) is one of the defining moments of a Japanese person's life, preceded by the infamous 受験 (じゅけん, entrance exam) period.

Related words: 大学生 (だいがくせい, university student). 大学院 (だいがくいん, graduate school). 短大 (たんだい, junior college — short for 短期大学). The abbreviation pattern is common: long compound → first kanji of each part.

The kanji 安 is a woman (女) under a roof (宀). The original meaning wasn't "cheap" — it was "peace, safety." A woman safe inside a house. (Whether this etymology is charmingly protective or uncomfortably patriarchal depends on your perspective.) The "cheap" meaning came later, as an extension: peaceful → settled → easy → easy on the wallet.

That original "peaceful" sense still lives: 安心 (あんしん, peace of mind). 安全 (あんぜん, safety). 不安 (ふあん, anxiety — not-peace). When you see 安 in compounds with the on'yomi あん, it usually means safety or ease. When you hear it as やすい, it means cheap.

Suffix alert: 〜やすい attached to a verb's masu-stem means "easy to do" — and it's one of the most useful suffixes in the language. 読みやすい (easy to read). 分かりやすい (easy to understand). 壊れやすい (easy to break, fragile). This やすい is written in hiragana and is technically a different word from the adjective 安い, but the sound-meaning connection is real: easy, not burdensome, low-effort.

もっと is the everyday, colloquial "more" — the one you reach for in conversation. もっと食べて (eat more). もっと大きい (bigger). もっと早く言って (you should have said so sooner — literally, say it more early). It intensifies whatever follows — more of the same quality, action, or degree.

Japanese doesn't have comparative adjective forms the way English does (no "-er" suffix). Instead, you just put もっと in front of the adjective: もっと安い (cheaper). もっと難しい (harder). Clean and consistent — no irregular comparatives, no "good/better/best" nightmares. Just もっと + any adjective.

もっと vs もう: もっと asks for a greater degree of the same thing. もう (+ a number/quantity) asks for an additional unit. もっと水 = more water (a larger amount). もう一杯 = another glass (one more of the same). The difference matters: もっと adjusts the dial. もう adds to the count.

The kanji 帰 has a complex history — the left component relates to a marching army, and the right (帚) is a broom. The original meaning was soldiers returning home, brooms sweeping the path for their arrival. Over time it simplified to just "return." But not any return — 帰る specifically means returning to where you belong. Home. Your country. Your base. The place where you're from.

This is the key distinction: 帰る vs 戻る (もどる). Both mean "return" but 帰る points homeward — the destination is your home base. 戻る means to go back to a previous location or state — any location. 家に帰る (go home). 席に戻る (return to your seat). You wouldn't say 席に帰る because your seat isn't "home."

帰りたい — "I want to go home" — might be the most relatable sentence in any language. In Japanese workplaces, announcing お先に失礼します (excuse me for leaving before you) is the ritual phrase for going home, because leaving before your colleagues requires an apology. 帰る is loaded with social weight in work culture.

Particles: 帰る takes に for the destination — 家に帰る (go home). It also takes から for where you're returning from: 学校から帰る (come home from school).

Passive に帰られる: indirect passive — someone going home affects you. 彼女に帰られた (she went home — and I was left behind / it affected me negatively). The に marks the person whose departure impacted you.

Causative を／に帰らせる: making or letting someone go home. を for coercive — 生徒を帰らせる (send the student home). に for permissive — 社員に早く帰らせる (let the employee go home early).

Here's the thing that will trip you up with 分かる: it's intransitive. In English, "I understand the problem" makes "I" the subject and "the problem" the object. In Japanese, the thing understood is the subject: 問題が分かる — "the problem is understandable (to me)." You don't do understanding. Understanding happens. The thing becomes clear, and you're just the person it happens to.

The kanji 分 shows a knife (刀) cutting something in two (八). To divide, to separate, to distinguish. 分かる is when things become separated — when the confusion splits apart and you can see clearly. That's a more precise image than English "understand" (which literally means "stand under"). Japanese understanding is an act of clarity, of things sorting themselves out.

分かる vs 知る: 知る is about information storage — do you have this fact? 分かる is about comprehension — does this make sense to you? 住所を知っている (I know the address — it's stored in my memory). この文法が分かる (I understand this grammar — it clicks for me). You 知る a phone number. You 分かる calculus.

Everyday use: 分かった is the past tense, but it functions as "got it" or "understood" in conversation — an acknowledgment, not a report about the past. Someone explains something, you say 分かった. It's one of the most common responses in daily Japanese.

Particles: 分かる takes が because the thing understood is the grammatical subject — it's the thing that "becomes clear." 日本語が分かる (Japanese is understandable to me). You are not acting on it; it is revealing itself to you.

Passive: Rarely used — 分かる is already structured like a passive in English. You might occasionally see に分かられる in the indirect sense (someone understanding something affecting you), but it's uncommon.

Causative を／に分からせる: making someone understand. に marks the person: 子供に分からせる (make the child understand). The thing being understood can take を.

広い is spaciousness in all directions. A 広い部屋 is a room with lots of floor space. A 広い道 is a wide road. 広い心 is a broad mind — generosity of spirit. The word doesn't care whether the space extends sideways, forward, or metaphorically. If there's room to breathe, it's 広い.

The kanji 広 has the shelter radical (广) — a roof with one side open, like a lean-to. Under that shelter sits ム, a component that in this context relates to an arm reaching outward. The image: a covered space that extends outward. Compare it with 大きい, which is about size in general. A 大きい部屋 is a big room (could be tall-ceilinged). A 広い部屋 is a spacious room (floor area, room to move). 大きい is volume. 広い is spread.

The verb form: 広がる (ひろがる, to spread out — intransitive) and 広げる (ひろげる, to spread something — transitive). The plain stretches out before you: 草原が広がっている. You unfold a map: 地図を広げる. These two verbs come up constantly and they grow directly from this adjective's root.

The kanji 数 is a busy one — it packs in 米 (rice), 女 (woman), and 攵 (a hand holding a stick). The oldest interpretation: a person with a stick, counting out grains of rice. Tallying. That image captures the word precisely — 数 isn't an abstract number like the concept of "seven." It's how many. Quantity. The count of things.

かず vs すう: Same kanji, two readings with different jobs. かず is the native Japanese reading, used as a standalone word: 数が多い (the number is large), 数を数える (count the number — yes, the same kanji appears twice with different readings: かずをかぞえる). すう is the Chinese-derived reading, used in compounds: 数学 (すうがく, mathematics), 数字 (すうじ, numeral/digit). When 数 stands alone meaning "quantity," it's always かず.

There's also a sneaky prefix use: 数〜 read as すう means "several" — 数日 (すうじつ, several days), 数回 (すうかい, several times). A vague, small number. Not one or two, not a dozen — somewhere in between. This prefix is everywhere in written Japanese.

The kanji 近 is the road radical (辶) plus 斤 (an axe). An axe is close range — you have to be right next to something to swing at it. The road radical adds the sense of approaching, getting to that close range. Together: nearness. Closeness you can reach.

近い works for both physical and abstract proximity. 駅に近い (close to the station). 答えに近い (close to the answer). 近い将来 (the near future). Notice the particle: に近い — you're close to something. The に marks what you're close to, not where you are.

近い vs 近く: The adverb form 近く doubles as a noun meaning "the vicinity" — 駅の近く (near the station / the area around the station). This is a common pattern with い-adjectives in Japanese, but 近く as a standalone place-noun gets particularly heavy use. この近くにコンビニある？ (is there a convenience store near here?) — one of the most useful sentences you can learn.

You've already seen これ and それ — そこ is the そ-series member for places. "There" — but specifically, there near you, the listener. Not far away in the distance (that's あそこ). Not here by me (that's ここ). The spot in your zone.

The three-way split: ここにある (it's here, by me). そこにある (it's there, by you). あそこにある (it's over there, away from both of us). English collapses the last two into "there" and relies on pointing or context. Japanese insists on knowing whose territory the place belongs to.

In conversation, そこ also refers to a place just mentioned — like それ can refer to a thing just mentioned. 昨日レストランに行った。そこで友達に会った。 (I went to a restaurant yesterday. I ran into a friend there.) The restaurant becomes そこ because it's shared information now — it entered your conversational territory.

The kanji 走 is a person (土 at top, a compressed figure) with legs pumping at the bottom (㔾, a foot in motion). In oracle bone script it was unmistakable — a full human figure with arms swinging and legs mid-stride. The modern form compressed it, but the energy is still there.

走る covers running in every sense. A person runs: 公園を走る. A car runs: 車が走る. A train runs along tracks: 電車が走る. Pain runs through your body: 痛みが走る. The common thread is rapid movement along a path. If something is covering ground quickly, it's 走る.

Sound anchor: ha-shi-ru — three sharp syllables that feel like feet hitting pavement. Fast, rhythmic, forward-moving. Say it quickly a few times and it starts to sound like someone sprinting.

Particles: 走る takes を to mark the path or surface — 道を走る (run along the road). This is the same "path を" that 出る uses — it doesn't mark a direct object but the route of movement. You can also use で for the location: 公園で走る (run in the park).

Passive に走られる: indirect passive — someone running affects you. 犬に走られて追いつけなかった (the dog ran and I couldn't keep up). に marks the runner.

Causative を／に走らせる: making or letting someone run. を is coercive — 生徒を走らせる (make the students run). に is permissive — 子供に走らせる (let the kid run).

The kanji 入 is one of the most visually transparent in the language — two strokes angling downward and inward, like something sliding into an opening. Oracle bone script showed it even more clearly: an arrowhead shape pointing into a space. 入れる is the transitive verb — you put something in. Its intransitive partner 入る (はいる) means something goes in by itself.

This pair — 入れる / 入る — is worth locking in. コーヒーに砂糖を入れる (put sugar in coffee — you do it). 部屋に入る (enter a room — you go in). The meaning of the kanji doesn't change. What changes is agency: who's doing the entering?

入れる is startlingly versatile. 電気を入れる (turn on the power — "put in" electricity). お茶を入れる (brew tea — "put" tea together). 力を入れる (put in effort). Each time, something is being placed inside or activated. The "put in" metaphor stretches far.

Particles: 入れる takes を for what you put in, and に for where you put it. 箱にりんごを入れる (put apples in the box). The に marks the container, the を marks the thing being inserted.

Potential が入れられる: が marks what can be put in. まだ荷物が入れられる (more luggage can still be put in).

Passive に入れられる: に marks who did the putting. コーヒーに砂糖を入れられた (someone put sugar in my coffee — and I didn't want it).

Causative に…を入れさせる: に marks who you make do the inserting. 子供にお金を貯金箱に入れさせる (make the kid put money in the piggy bank).

The kanji 教 is 孝 (filial piety, or more literally a child learning from an elder) plus 攵 (a hand holding a rod). The image: someone with a stick, guiding a younger person. Teaching in its oldest, most physical sense — an elder transmitting knowledge, with a rod to keep attention focused. The modern word has softened, but that directional relationship remains: 教える always flows from someone who knows to someone who doesn't.

Two meanings, one verb: 教える means both "to teach" and "to tell/inform." 英語を教える (teach English). 道を教える (tell someone the way). 電話番号を教えて (tell me your phone number). The second meaning surprises English speakers — you're not "teaching" a phone number, but Japanese sees sharing information as the same act. You have knowledge; you transmit it. That's 教える.

Sound: o-shi-e-ru — four syllables, the longest verb you've hit so far. The おし at the start has weight to it, like clearing your throat before an explanation. Appropriate for a word about passing on knowledge.

Particles: 教える takes を for what you teach and に for who you teach. 生徒に数学を教える (teach students math). The person receiving knowledge takes に — they're the destination of the information.

Potential が教えられる: が marks the subject that can be taught. この科目が教えられる (this subject can be taught).

Passive に教えられる: に marks the teacher. 先生に大事なことを教えられた (I was taught something important by the teacher).

Causative に教えさせる: に marks who you make teach. 先輩に新人を教えさせる (have the senior teach the newcomer).

The kanji 歩 is 止 (foot, stop) on top of 少 (few/small). But the original form told a different story: it was two feet, one behind the other, alternating. Left foot, right foot. The motion of walking encoded directly in the character. The top 止 was one footprint; the bottom was the other. Over centuries the bottom half morphed into something resembling 少, but the walking is still there if you know to look.

歩く vs 散歩する: 歩く is walking as locomotion — getting from A to B on foot. 散歩する (さんぽする) is a stroll, a walk for pleasure. 駅まで歩く (walk to the station — transportation). 公園を散歩する (take a walk in the park — leisure). If you're going somewhere, it's 歩く. If you're wandering, it's 散歩.

The sound a-ru-ku has a plodding, step-by-step rhythm. Three even syllables, each one a footfall. Compare with 走る (はしる) — sharper, faster syllables. The sounds match the speeds.

Particles: 歩く takes を to mark the path — 道を歩く (walk along the road). This is route-marking を, not direct object を. You can also use で for the location: 公園で歩く.

Passive に歩かれる: indirect passive. 隣の人に部屋の中を歩かれて眠れなかった (the neighbor walked around in their room and I couldn't sleep). に marks the walker.

Causative を／に歩かせる: を is coercive — 兵士を歩かせる (make the soldiers walk). に is permissive — 赤ちゃんに歩かせる (let the baby walk).

The kanji 会 is a roof or lid (亼) over a mouth or opening. The oldest meaning: things coming together under one place, converging. A meeting. Not a coincidence that the same kanji appears in 会議 (かいぎ, meeting/conference), 社会 (しゃかい, society — people gathered together), and 会社 (かいしゃ, company — a gathering-place for work).

会う is intransitive — meeting is something that happens between people, not something one person does to another. That's why it takes に, not を. You don't meet someone like you'd hit a ball. You go toward them and converge: 友達に会う.

会う vs 合う: Both are あう, and they share the convergence idea, but 会う is people meeting while 合う is things matching or fitting. 友達に会う (meet a friend). サイズが合う (the size fits). Different kanji, same sound, related but distinct concepts. When two people come together, 会. When two things come together, 合.

Particles: 会う takes に — you move toward someone and meet them. 先生に会う (meet the teacher). The に marks the person as the destination of your movement, just like に with 行く. You can also use と to emphasize mutuality: 友達と会う (meet with a friend — both actively meeting).

Passive に会われる: rare and usually indirect. More commonly, the passive idea is expressed differently in Japanese for this verb.

Causative を／に会わせる: making or letting someone meet. 二人を会わせる (introduce two people — make them meet). 娘に彼氏を会わせる (let my daughter meet her boyfriend).

The kanji 書 is layered: a hand holding a brush (聿) over 曰 (to speak/say). A brush that speaks — writing as speech made visible. The earliest Chinese writing was done with brushes, and this kanji preserves that. Even though you probably write with a pen or a keyboard now, 書く carries that old connection between hand and language.

書く covers both writing and drawing, though for serious artwork Japanese usually switches to 描く (かく or えがく). 手紙を書く (write a letter). 名前を書く (write your name). 絵を描く (draw a picture — same pronunciation, different kanji for the artistic version). In casual usage, 書く handles both.

Conjugation note: 書く is a く-ending godan verb, so its て-form is 書いて and its past is 書いた — the く drops and becomes い. This is the standard pattern for く-verbs (unlike 行く → 行って, which is the sole exception).

Particles: 書く takes を — the thing written is your direct object. 小説を書く (write a novel). Use に or で for the surface or instrument: 紙に書く (write on paper), ペンで書く (write with a pen).

Potential が書ける: が — what can be written becomes the subject. 漢字が書ける (I can write kanji).

Passive に書かれる: に marks the writer. 新聞に書かれた (it was written in the newspaper).

Causative に…を書かせる: に marks who you make write. 生徒に作文を書かせる (make the students write an essay).

The kanji 頭 has 豆 (bean) on the left and 頁 (head/page) on the right. 頁 is the real clue — it originally depicted a human head, and it appears in every kanji related to the head and face: 顔 (かお, face), 額 (ひたい, forehead), 顎 (あご, chin). If you see 頁 on the right side of a kanji, odds are good it's about something on your head.

あたま is the physical head, but it also means intelligence: 頭がいい (smart — "good head"), 頭が悪い (stupid — "bad head"). The equation of head with brain is universal, but Japanese wears it more openly. You don't say someone is "bright" or "sharp" — you just rate their head directly.

頭 vs 頭: This kanji has multiple readings. あたま is the everyday word for your physical head. とう is a counter for large animals: 牛三頭 (three head of cattle). ず appears in compounds like 頭痛 (ずつう, headache). Same kanji, three different lives depending on context.

売 is 士 (scholar/samurai) over 買's old components — but the simplest way to read it: the top is a display stand, the bottom is legs walking. Something set up for people to come and take away. Selling. And because you already know 買う (かう, to buy), lock them in as a pair: 買う and 売る, two sides of every transaction.

売る is transitive — you sell something. 車を売る. 家を売る. Its intransitive partner is 売れる (うれる) — something sells, it moves off the shelves on its own: この本はよく売れている (this book sells well). The distinction matters: 売る is your decision to sell. 売れる is the market's response.

An idiom worth knowing: 喧嘩を売る (けんかをうる, to pick a fight — literally "sell a fight"). You're offering a fight to someone, putting it out there for them to "buy." And the response? 喧嘩を買う — "buying" the fight, accepting the challenge. The buy/sell metaphor maps perfectly onto provocation and response.

Particles: 売る takes を for what you sell. 本を売る. Add に for the buyer: 友達に車を売る (sell the car to a friend).

Potential が売れる: が — what can be sold becomes the subject. Note: 売れる also exists independently as the intransitive "to sell (well)," so context matters.

Passive に売られる: に marks the seller. 友達に売られた can mean "was sold by a friend" — and in some contexts, "was betrayed by a friend."

Causative に…を売らせる: に marks who you make sell, を marks the goods. 店員に商品を売らせる (have the clerk sell the product).

大 (big) + 好き (like) = 大好き. Big-like. Love. It's that direct. Japanese intensifies adjectives and na-adjectives by slapping 大 in front: 大嫌い (だいきらい, hate), 大事 (だいじ, important). This prefix goes back centuries and it's still productive today.

大好き is how you confess. Not in a casual "I love pizza" way — though it works for that too — but in the dramatic, heart-pounding, standing-in-the-rain confession that defines Japanese romance narratives. 大好きです, said to someone's face, is a love confession. It's the word that appears at the climax of every shoujo manga, every drama confession scene. 愛してる (あいしてる, I love you) exists but is almost too intense for daily use — married couples go years without saying it. 大好き is the sweet spot: deep feeling, but not so heavy it crushes.

For things and activities, 大好き is enthusiastic love: 猫が大好き (I love cats). ラーメンが大好き. 日本が大好き. Grammatically it works exactly like 好き — the thing liked takes が, and 大好き sits at the end.

The kanji 体 is a simplified form of 體, which had 骨 (bone) on the left — the body understood through its skeleton, its structural core. The modern form swapped in 本 (root, origin) next to the person radical: a person's root, their fundamental physical self. Either way, 体 is the body as a whole system, not a collection of parts.

からだ doesn't distinguish between the body you exercise and the body you inhabit. 体が強い (physically strong). 体に気をつけて (take care of yourself — literally "pay attention to your body"). 体の調子が悪い (feeling unwell — "the body's condition is bad"). Japanese talks about health through the body far more directly than English does. You don't "feel sick" — your body's condition is off.

Readings: からだ is the standalone word. In compounds, it's たい: 体力 (たいりょく, physical strength), 体重 (たいじゅう, body weight), 体温 (たいおん, body temperature). The compound reading たい shows up constantly in medical and health contexts.

The kanji 直 means straight, direct, honest — a line without bends. すぐ takes that directness and applies it to time: no gap, no delay, straight from now to the action. すぐ行く (I'll go right away). すぐ来る (coming right away). すぐ終わる (it'll be over soon).

But すぐ also works for space: 駅のすぐ近く (right near the station). すぐそこ (right there — very close). The word means "without distance" in either dimension — no gap in time, no gap in space. A straight line from here to there, from now to then.

In practice, すぐ is rarely written with its kanji. You'll almost always see it in hiragana. The kanji 直 gets more use in its other readings: 直す (なおす, to fix/correct — make straight), 正直 (しょうじき, honest — correct and straight). But the underlying concept — directness, no detours — runs through every use of this character.

The kanji 飛 is one of the most visually expressive in the language. Look at it: those two wing-like strokes fanning out on the right, the compact body on the left. It's a bird mid-flight, caught in a single character. In oracle bone script, it was even more obviously a bird with outspread wings. The modern form streamlined it, but the flight is still visible.

飛ぶ means both "to fly" and "to jump" — movement that leaves the ground. 鳥が飛ぶ (a bird flies). ページが飛ぶ (a page is skipped — it "flies" over). 噂が飛ぶ (rumors fly/spread). The common thread: something leaves its position and moves through space rapidly. For specifically jumping as an athletic action, Japanese often uses 跳ぶ (same reading, different kanji), but in casual writing 飛ぶ covers both.

Sound: to-bu — two syllables, light and quick, like something lifting off. The ぶ ending gives it a slight buzz, like the hum of wings.

Particles: 飛ぶ takes を to mark the space traveled through — 空を飛ぶ (fly through the sky). This is route-marking を. For destination, use に or へ: 南へ飛ぶ (fly south).

Passive に飛ばれる: rare. Indirect passive — 鳥に飛ばれた (the bird flew away on me).

Causative を／に飛ばせる: を is coercive, に is permissive. But more commonly, the transitive verb 飛ばす (とばす, to make fly, to skip) is used instead of the causative form.

とても is the standard, all-purpose intensifier — "very" in its most neutral form. とてもおいしい (very delicious). とても大きい (very big). とても好き (like a lot). It sits right before the adjective or adverb it modifies. No complications.

Hidden second life: とても with a negative verb means "not at all possible" or "by no means" — a completely different nuance. とてもできない (I absolutely can't do it). とても間に合わない (there's no way we'll make it in time). This usage is the word's original meaning — とても started as "no matter what" or "in any case," and the "very" meaning developed later. You'll see the negative-pairing version more in written Japanese and older speech.

Neighborhood: とても vs すごく vs 非常に. All mean "very," but they live at different register levels. とても is neutral — fine in conversation, fine in writing. すごく is casual and emphatic — a friend gushing about food. 非常に (ひじょうに) is formal — news reports, academic writing. For most situations as a learner, とても is the safe pick.

The kanji 誰 has 言 (speech) on the left and 隹 (a short-tailed bird) on the right. The bird component here is borrowed for its sound, not its meaning — but you can construct a visual anchor anyway: a voice calling out, "who goes there?" Like a bird-call of identification. Asking someone to name themselves.

だれ follows the same shapeshifting pattern as いつ (when). Add particles and it transforms:

だれも → everyone (+ positive verb) / nobody (+ negative verb)
だれか → someone, somebody
だれでも → anyone, everybody

Watch the も version — it flips meaning based on the verb. だれもいる (everyone is here) vs だれもいない (nobody is here). Same word, opposite meanings, controlled entirely by whether the verb is positive or negative. This catches people off guard, but it's the same logic as English "anybody" ("did anybody come?" vs "nobody came").

The polite form is どなた — use it when asking about or referring to someone you should be respectful toward. どなたですか (who might you be?) vs だれ？ (who?). The register difference is significant.

Here's the thing about 好き that will reshape how you think about Japanese: it's not a verb. English says "I like cats." Japanese says 猫が好き — "cats are likeable (to me)." The cats are the subject (が). You are not grammatically present. Liking is framed as a quality of the thing liked, not an action performed by the liker.

The kanji 好 is 女 (woman) and 子 (child). A woman with a child — the image of affection, of something precious held close. Whether you read this as maternal love, as fondness, or simply as "what humans naturally care about," the emotional root is warmth and attachment.

Usage range: 好き covers everything from mild preference to serious romantic feeling. コーヒーが好き (I like coffee). あの人が好き (I like/am attracted to that person). Context and tone determine the intensity. In a romantic confession, 好きです alone can carry the full weight of "I have feelings for you" — no need for 大好き or 愛. The simplicity is what makes it powerful.

Because 好き takes が, the thing liked often seems to come first. 何が好き？ (what do you like?). 音楽が好き (I like music). Get used to this reversed-feeling structure — it's the foundation for all the other "feeling" adjectives in Japanese.

The kanji 読 has 言 (speech/words) on the left and 売 on the right — but 売 here is phonetic, not semantic. What matters is the left side: words. Reading is the act of receiving words. And the original meaning was even more specific: to read aloud. In ancient China, reading was not a silent activity. You voiced the text, gave it breath. That older meaning survives in 句読点 (くとうてん, punctuation marks — literally "phrase-reading marks").

読む extends beyond text. 空気を読む (read the room — literally "read the air") is one of the most culturally loaded phrases in Japanese. The ability to 空気を読む — to sense the unspoken mood and adjust your behavior — is considered a fundamental social skill. Someone who can't is called KY (ケーワイ, short for 空気読めない). The verb went from reading text to reading people.

Particles: 読む takes を — the text is your direct object. 本を読む (read a book). 新聞を読む (read the newspaper).

Potential が読める: が marks what can be read. 漢字が読める (I can read kanji) — one of the most satisfying sentences you'll eventually say.

Passive に読まれる: に marks the reader. 日記を母に読まれた (my diary was read by my mother) — classic adversative passive.

Causative に…を読ませる: に marks who you make read. 生徒に本を読ませる (have students read a book).

The kanji 次 is 冫 (ice/cold radical) plus 欠 (lacking, yawning — a person with mouth open). The etymology is debated, but one reading: breath becoming visible in cold air — one puff after another, sequential. Another: the 冫 is phonetic, and the core meaning is simply "what follows after a gap." Either way, the word means the one that comes after.

次 works as both a noun and a prefix. As a noun: 次は何？ (what's next?). 次の電車 (the next train). 次の日 (the next day). It always needs の when modifying a noun — 次電車 doesn't work. This marks it as a true noun, not an adjective.

In compounds: 次 read as じ means "order" or "secondary": 次第 (しだい, depends on / gradually), 目次 (もくじ, table of contents — literally "eye-order"). These are worth recognizing because the reading shifts completely from つぎ to じ in Chinese-compound territory.

Learn this word. Then learn to almost never use it.

あなた is the textbook "you," and it's technically correct. But in actual conversation, saying あなた to someone's face can sound cold, confrontational, or condescending — like poking them with a stick. Japanese avoids second-person pronouns the way English avoids calling your boss by the wrong title. Instead, you use their name or title: 田中さんはどう思いますか (what do you think, Tanaka-san?). Or you just drop the subject entirely: どう思う？ (what do you think?).

The exceptions are specific. A wife calling her husband あなた is an established term of endearment — warm, domestic, like English "dear." In surveys, forms, and instructions, あなた is standard because there's no specific person to name. And in song lyrics, あなた appears constantly as a poetic "you" — intimate, often longing.

The kanji, when written, is 貴方 — "precious direction," an honorific way of saying "you" that has been worn down by centuries of use into something far less honorific. Most of the time, it's written in hiragana.

The kanji 飲 has 食 (eat/food) on the left, compressed into its radical form, and 欠 (a person with mouth open) on the right. A person opening their mouth near food — but specifically for liquids. The 食 radical links it to the whole family of consumption kanji, while the open mouth on the right distinguishes it from solid food.

飲む covers all drinking: 水を飲む, お茶を飲む, ビールを飲む. But it extends to swallowing things that aren't drinks: 薬を飲む (take medicine — you "drink" pills in Japanese). If it goes down your throat, it's 飲む. This catches English speakers off guard with medicine — you don't "take" or "eat" pills in Japanese, you drink them.

飲む vs 飲める: お酒が飲める (I can drink alcohol) is how you say you're of drinking age, or that you tolerate alcohol well. 飲める？ directed at someone is a common way to ask "do you drink?" — not as a question about current activity, but about capability and preference.

Particles: 飲む takes を — the liquid is your direct object. ジュースを飲む.

Potential が飲める: が marks what can be drunk. この水が飲める (this water is drinkable).

Passive に飲まれる: に marks the drinker. 最後の一本を弟に飲まれた (my little brother drank the last one on me). Classic adversative passive.

Causative に…を飲ませる: に marks who you make drink. 子供に薬を飲ませる (make the child take medicine).

The kanji 古 is 十 (ten) over 口 (mouth). Ten generations of mouths passing down stories — that which has been spoken of for ages. Old. The kanji itself has been in use for thousands of years, which makes it a kind of self-referential artifact.

Critical distinction: 古い is for things, never for people. An old building, an old book, an old tradition — all 古い. An old person? Never. For people's age, Japanese uses separate vocabulary entirely. Calling someone 古い doesn't mean "elderly" — it means they're outdated, behind the times. その考え方は古い (that way of thinking is old-fashioned). It's a criticism, not a description.

古い pairs naturally with 新しい (あたらしい, new). Old temple, new temple. Old car, new car. But 古い can carry positive weight too: 古い町並み (old townscape) evokes charm and history. 古い友達 (old friend) means a friend you've known a long time — warmth, not decay. Context determines whether "old" is a flaw or a virtue.

Two kanji: 質 (quality, substance, nature) and 問 (to ask, question). A question about the substance of something — asking to understand the nature of a thing. The word implies a purposeful, structured question, not idle wondering. 質問がある (I have a question) is how you raise your hand in class or interrupt a presentation. It's the formal, deliberate act of asking.

質問 vs 聞く: 聞く (きく) is the everyday verb for asking — casual, quick, built into the flow of conversation. 質問する is more deliberate: you're formally posing a question, requesting information. ちょっと聞いていい？ (can I ask you something?) is casual. 質問してもいいですか (may I ask a question?) is raising your hand in a meeting.

The kanji 問 is a mouth (口) inside a gate (門) — a voice at the gate, calling out to ask for entry. Asking to be let in, to gain access. Every question is a knock on a door of knowledge. You'll see 問 in 問題 (もんだい, problem/question), 疑問 (ぎもん, doubt/question), 訪問 (ほうもん, visit — knocking on someone's gate).

今 (now) + 日 (day) = today. The logic is transparent. But the reading isn't: きょう has nothing to do with いま (now) + ひ (day). It's a native Japanese word that got mapped onto these Chinese characters. The regular compound reading こんにち also exists — you hear it every day in こんにちは, which literally means "as for this day" (a greeting that was originally the start of a longer sentence).

きょう is a time word, which in Japanese means it doesn't need a particle to indicate when something happens. 今日は暑い (it's hot today). 今日、学校に行く (I'm going to school today). No に needed — 今日 functions as its own time marker.

The time trio: 昨日 (きのう, yesterday), 今日 (きょう, today), 明日 (あした, tomorrow). All three have irregular readings — the kanji say one thing, the pronunciation says another. This is because these are among the oldest, most frequently used words in the language, and ancient native readings stuck even as kanji were adopted. Learn them as units, not as kanji combinations.

友 is two hands reaching in the same direction — people working together, moving together. The oldest oracle bone forms showed two right hands side by side. Same direction, same purpose. That's what friendship was, etymologically: alignment. The kanji 達 means "to reach, to achieve" and also functions as a pluralizer in some contexts. Together, 友達 is your fellow-reachers, the people alongside you.

友達 is the standard, all-purpose word for friend. It's not formal, not casual — it's the default. 友達に会う (meet a friend). 友達ができた (I made a friend — literally "a friend came into being"). Notice that last one: you don't "make" friends in Japanese. Friends happen. できる (to come into existence) is how Japanese frames the emergence of a friendship — it's not something you manufacture, it's something that forms.

Neighborhood: 友達 (general friend), 親友 (しんゆう, close/best friend — "intimate friend"), 知り合い (しりあい, acquaintance — someone you know), 仲間 (なかま, comrade/companion — someone in your group). The line between 友達 and 知り合い matters socially: Japanese people can be particular about who qualifies as a 友達 vs a 知り合い.

The kanji 早 is a sun (日) rising above a horizon line. The first light. Early morning. The word 早い means early — 早い時間 (early hour), 朝早い (early in the morning) — but it also means fast, quick: 返事が早い (quick to respond). The connection between "early" and "fast" makes intuitive sense: something that arrives early is something that moved quickly.

早い vs 速い: Both read はやい. Both can mean "fast." The difference: 早い is about timing (early, soon, premature). 速い is about speed (rapid, swift). 朝が早い (morning is early — timing). 車が速い (the car is fast — speed). In practice, the distinction blurs and 早い gets used for both in casual writing. But when precision matters — especially in writing — the split is real.

The compound 早速 (さっそく, immediately/at once) literally combines both kanji — early + speed — and is one of those words that sounds crisp and decisive in conversation. 早速始めましょう (let's get started right away).

どれ is the question member of the ko-so-a-do system for standalone things: これ (this one), それ (that one near you), あれ (that one over there), どれ (which one?). The ど prefix turns any demonstrative into a question — どこ (where?), どの (which?), どれ (which thing?).

どれ vs どっち vs どの: These three all ask "which?" but in different situations. どっち (or the polite どちら) is for choosing between two options: コーヒーとお茶、どっち？ (coffee or tea, which?). どれ is for three or more options: どれがいい？ (which one do you want?) with multiple choices laid out. どの goes before a noun: どの色？ (which color?). Get the two-vs-three distinction down and the rest sorts itself out.

Just like the other question words, どれ shapeshifts with particles: どれも (all of them / none of them + negative), どれか (one of them, some of them), どれでも (any of them, whichever). The pattern is identical to だれ and いつ — same particles, same logic.
